statement: A hybrid phased migration that first removes global scan hotspots (indexed request lookups + partitioned due-work queues) while retaining workflow handlers and lease/idempotency guards will achieve materially higher throughput with lower correctness risk than either a full scheduler-only rewrite or a full queue-only rewrite in one step.
rationale: The current system already combines workflow handlers with scheduler fan-out and lease claims, so the highest near-term risk is scan/read amplification rather than missing orchestration primitives. Convex limits and component ceilings favor bounded, partitioned fan-out and small payload workflows over global polling loops.
keyAssumptions: Run/evidence cardinality will continue to grow enough that current status-scan patterns become a dominant bottleneck
Existing request apply paths remain idempotent under mixed-trigger migration
Provider-side rate limits remain the external bottleneck after internal scan pressure is reduced
validationCriteria: After Phase 1, p95 scheduler tick duration and per-tick docs/bytes read decrease by at least 40% at comparable load
After Phase 2, throughput (completed requests/minute) increases by at least 2x without increasing duplicate-apply or stage-regression incidents
After Phase 3, stage advancement no longer requires full-status scans and remains correct under retry/failure tests
Pending scheduled function counts and component function-handle usage stay below Convex limits under stress
critiquePoints: Counter-based progression can introduce subtle drift bugs if invariants are incomplete
Hybrid operation temporarily increases codepath complexity until old scan paths are removed
Observed gains may be muted if provider limits dominate earlier than expected
uncertainties: Current production-scale table cardinalities and true scan-cost contribution to p95 latency
Optimal shard key strategy for partitioned due-work queues
Whether workflow max parallelism settings or provider limits become first-order constraints post-migration
questions: What concrete throughput target (requests/minute and concurrent runs) defines success for migration phases?
Should Phase 2 queues be sharded by run_id, experiment_id, or time bucket for best fairness and hot-spot control?
What rollback mechanism will disable new queue triggers quickly if duplicate processing appears?
